name: residual add (the on-chip FFN/attention residual step)

op.signature: out[M,K] = (x.float() + residual.float()).to(dtype), fp32 promotion then cast

preconditions: x, residual share shape [M,K] and are contiguous row-major
the add is promoted to fp32 then cast to the input dtype (matches xkernels.ops.comm.fused.add_rmsnorm_ref's residual convention); a fused megakernel stage performs the same promotion on-chip

numerics.notes: Pure pointwise binary add. There is NO reduction, so reduce_dtype is intentionally absent — the fp32 promotion is a precision choice (matches the existing residual op xkernels.ops.comm.fused.add_rmsnorm_ref: hf = x.float() + residual.float(); out = hf.to(x.dtype)), not an accumulation. The op exists as a FIRST-CLASS CONTRACT so a persistent-grid megakernel can name the residual step as an on-chip stage with a defined precision path instead of hand-waving 'just add them' (megakernel-blockers.md (b) point 4). A standalone Triton kernel is deliberately NOT shipped: a separate launch for x+residual would LOSE to torch.add (launch overhead > the add), so the reference IS the implementation for a standalone call, and the contract is what a fused card references. This is the 'compose over generate' stance (library.md §1.2) — don't emit a kernel for what torch.add already does.
